concept.pre:
Atmospheric pressure is defined as the result of gravity acting on the mass
of the earth's atmosphere. Although the mass of the atmosphere is evenly
spread over the earth's surface, differences in heating and cooling in the
lower levels of the atmosphere, where most of the mass is concentrated,
cause density variations which in turn cause small horizontal pressure
differences. These pressure differences are only about one ten thousandth
the magnitude of the normal change of pressure with altitude; but, they are
still the cause of all atmospheric circulation and most weather phenomena.
229: Every physical process of weather is accompanied by, or is the result
of:
A heat exchange The movement of air A pressure differential Moisture 230: Winds at 5000 feet AGL on a particular flight are southwesterly while
most of the surface winds are southerly. This difference in direction is
primarily due to:
Friction between the wind and the surface. A stronger pressure gradient at higher altitudes. Stronger coriolis force at the surface. The influence of pressure systems at the lower altitudes. 231: What are the processes by which moisuture is added to unsaturated air?
Evaporation and sublimation. Heading and sublimation. Heating and condensation. Supersaturation and evaporation. 232: The amount of water vapor which air can hold largely depends on:
Air temperature The dew point Stability of air Relative humidity

When the air is cooled to saturation, it is said to have reached the dew
point temperature. This takes its name from the fact that is is the
temperature where moisture first starts to condense on exposed surfaces or
forms dew. Aviation weather reports include the air temperature and dew
point temperature. Dew point when related to air temperature reveals
qualitatively how close the air is to saturation.
233: What is meant by the term dew point?
The temperature to which air must be cooled to become saturated. The temperature at which condensation and evaporation are equal. The temperature at which dew will always form. The spread between actual temperature and the temperature during evaporation.

A good indication of how close the air is to saturation is the temperature
- dew point spread. This is the numerical difference, in degrees, between
the temperature and the dew point. As the temperature - dew point spread
approaches zero, the relative humidity approaches 100%. When the dew point
depression has decreased to 4 degrees farenheight, it is said to be at the
critical spread. Any temperature - dew point trend toward the critical
spread must be watched closely for the development of visibility
restrictions.
Dew point is of tremendous significance to the pilot because it represents
a critical condition of the air. When temperature reaches the dew point,
water vapor can no longer remain invisible, but is forced to condense,
becoming visible on the ground as dew or frost, appearing in the air as fog
or clouds.
234: Clouds, fog, or dew will always form when:
Water vapor condenses. Water vapor is present. Relative humidity reaches or exceeds 100%. The temperature and dew point are equal. 235: If the temperature/dew point spread is small and decreasing, and the
temperature is 62 degrees farenheight, what type weather is most likely to
develop?
Fog or low clouds. Freezing precipitation. Thunderstorms. Rain showers.

Classification of clouds. Cloud types are initially classified according
to altitude and stability. The atmosphere has been divided into four
altitude regimes, or estages (From the "french" for floors), where clouds
have been found to have similar characteristics. Low clouds are found
between the surface and 6500 feet, middle clouds between 6500 feet and
18000 feet, and high clouds above 18000 feet and clouds with extensive
vertical development.
236: Clouds are divided into four families according to their:
Height range. Origin. Outward shape. Composition.

Within the high, middle, and low cloud groups are two main subdivisions.
These are stratiform (Stable) and cumuliform (Unstable). Stratus clouds
form when complete layers of air are cooled until condensation takes place
creating a "spread-out" or "sheet-like" appearance. Flight within
stratiform clouds is relatively smooth. Cumulus clouds form when localized
vertical currents carry most air upward to the condensation level creating
a "lumpy" or "billowy" appearnace. Turbulent flying conditions are usually
found in and below cumuliform clouds.
237: Moist stable air flowing upslope can be expected to:
Produce stratus type clouds. Produce a temperature inversion. Cause showers and thunderstorms. Develop convective turbulence. 238: If an unstable air mass is forced upward, what type clouds can be
expected?
Clouds with considerable vertical development and associated turbulence. Layer-like clouds with a temperature inversion. Layer-like clouds with little vertical development. Layer-like clouds with considerable associated turbulence.

Towering cumulus signifies a relatively deep layer of unstable air. It
shows considerable vertical development and has billowing cauliflower tops.
239: Which of the following cloud types would indicated convective
turbulence?
Towering cumulus clouds. Altocumulus standing lenticular clouds. Nimobostratus clouds. Cirrus clouds.

In addition to the two main subdivisions discussed above, the word nimbus,
meaning "rain cloud", is added to the names of clouds that normally produce
heavy precipitation, either liquid or solid. For example, a stratiform
cloud producing precipitation is referred to as a numbostratus and a heavy,
swelling cumulus cloud that has grown into a thunderstorm is referred to as
a cumulonimbus.
240: The suffix nimbus, used in naming clouds, means:
Rain cloud. Cloud with extensive vertical development. Middle cloud containing ice pellets. Accumulcation of clouds.

Cumulonimbus are heavy and dense clouds with considerable vertical extent
in the form of mountains or huge towers. They may be isolated, may appear
in groupds or may appear in lines extending hundreds of miles. At least
part of their upward portion is usually smooth, fibrous, or strated, the
shape of an anvil or vast plume. Under the base of this type cloud, which
is often very dark, there are frequently low ragged clouds, either merged
or not. The intensity of precipitation varies from light showers to
cloudbursts. Cumulonimbus produces lightning, thunder, gusty winds, and
often hail.
241: Which clouds have the greatest turbulence?
Cumulonimbus Towering cumulus Nimbostratus Altocumulus castellanus

Cloud base determination. You can estimate height of cumuliform cloud
bases using surface temperature-dew point spread. Unsaturated air in a
convective current cools at a rate of 5.4 degrees farenheight (3.0C) per
thousand feet; dew point decreases at about 1 degree farenheight (5/9C).
Thus in a convective current, temperature and dew converge at about 4.4
degrees farenheight (2.5C) per thousand feet. To obtain an estimate of a
convective cloud base in thousands of feet, divide the temperature/dew
point spread spread by the rate of convergence (4.4"F or 2.4"C).
242: At approximately what altitude above the surface would the pilot
expect the base of cumuliform clouds if the surface air temperature is 82'F
and the dew point is 51'F.
7000 feet MSL. 5000 feet MSL. 6000 feet MSL. 9000 feet MSL. 243: What is the approximate base of the cumulus clouds if the temperature
at 1000 feet MSL is 70"F and the dew point is 48"F.
6000 feet MSL. 3000 feet MSL. 4000 feet MSL. 8000 feet MSL. 244: What situation is mode conducive to the formation of radition fog?
Warm, moist air over low, flatland areas on clear, calm nights. Moist, tropical air moving over cold, offshore water. The movement of cold air over much warmer water. Light wind moving warm, moist air upslope during the night.

Advection fog. The cause of advection fog formation is the movement of
warm, moist air over a colder surface. Advection fog is common along
coastal regions where the temperature of the land surface and the water
surface contrasts. The southeastern area of the United States provides
ideal conditions for advection fog formation during the winter months. If
air flows (advection) from the Gulf of Mexico or the Atlantic Ocean over
the colder continent, this warm air is cooled by contact with the cold
ground. If the temperature of the air is lowered to the dew point
temperature, fog will form. Advection fog forming under these conditions
may extend over the entire eastern two-thirds of the nation from the
Rockies to Maine. It may persist both day and night until replaced by a
drier air mass.
If advection fog forms over water and is carried onshore by the wind, it is
often referred to as a "sea fog". Cold ocean currents, such as those off
the coast of California, may cool and saturate moist air coming from the
warmer areas of the open sea. Sea fog is often very dense both offshore
and onshore.
245: In which situation is advection fog most likely to form?
An air mass moving inland from the coast in winter. A warm moist air mass on the windward side of the mountains. A light breeze blowing colder air out to sea. Warm, moist air settling over a warmer surface under no-wind conditions.

Upslope fog. Upslope fog forms when moist, stable air flows up a sloping
land surface. When the air rises, it cools by expansion (adiabatic
cooling) as the atmospheric pressure decreases. When the expansional
cooling is sufficient to lower the temperature of the air to the dew point
temperature, upslope fog may form. The windspeed (pressure gradient) must
be adequate to support continued upslope motion; However, if the wind is
too strong, the fog may be lifted from the surface, resulting in an
overcast of low stratus clouds. Upslope fog is common on the eastern slope
of the Rockies as air flows westward from the Missouri Valley or the Gulf
of Mexico.
246: What types of fog depend upon a wind in order to exist?
Advection fog and upslope fog. Radiation fog and ice fog. Steam fog and downslope fog. Precipitation - induced fog and ground fog.

Temperature inversions. Temperature normally decreases with increasing
altitude throughout the troposphere. This decrease of temperature with
altitude is defined as lapse rate. The average decrease of temperature -
average lapse rate - in the troposphere is 2"c per 1000 feet. But since
this is an average, the exact value seldom exists. In fact, temperature
sometimes increases with height through a layer. When the temperature
increases with altitude within a layer in the atmosphere this layer is
called an inversion (inverted lapse rate).
247: A temperature inversion would most likely result in which of the
following weather conditions?
An increase in temperature as altitude is increased. Clouds with extensive vertical development above an inversion aloft. Good visibility in the lower levels of atmosphere and poor visibility above an inversion aloft.
A decrease in temperature as altitude is increased.

Inversions vary in thickness from a few feet to several thousand feet and
may occur in a layer of the troposphere at any altitude from the surface to
the stratosphere. Inversions occur frequently, but are generally
restricted to relatively small layers in the atmosphere. Inversions near
the earth's surface may be formed as follows:
Advection of Warm Air Over a Colder Surface. Wind may move a mass of air
from a warm region over a colder surface, such as a wind from the warm
ocean moving over the colder land area during the winter season. The air
will be cooled quickly at the surface by contact with the cold land mass,
while the air at and above 1000 feet will not be affected for several
hours. Thus, the surface air will be colder than the air aloft, producing
an inverted temperature lapse rate.
Nocturnal cooling. A more frequent type of inversion is caused when the
earth cools at night under clear skies. During the afternoon hours, the
ground is waqrm and a normal decrease in temperature with altitude
generally exists. However, as the sun sets, the ground loses its heat to
space through terrestrial radiation. This reradiation is not absorbed in
significant quantities by clear, dry air. As the ground becomes colder,
the air immediately above it is cooed by contact (conduction); Since air is
a poor conductor, this cooling does not extend very high. The air near
tghe surface may become cool by midnight hours, whereas the temperature of
the air at 100 feet may still be as warm as it was during the afternoon
hours, thus inverting the typical afternoon temperature lapse rate. As the
cooling continues, the inversion layer deepens.
248: The most frequent type of ground or surface based temperature inversion is
that produced by:
Terrestrial radiation on a clear, relatively still night. Warm air being lifted rapidly aloft in the vicinity of mountainous terrain.
The movement of colder air under warm air, or the movement of warm air over cold air.
Widespread sinking of air within a thich layer aloft resulting in heating by compression.

With relatively strong winds over land, the mechanical turbulence from air
moving over the rough surface may thoroughly mix the warm and cool air near
the surface so that no well-defined inversion exists near the ground,
although an inversion may exist several hundred feet above the ground.
Inversion layers are stable and produce smooth flying conditions with them;
however, fog, haze, smoke, and dust below and within inversion layers often
restrict visibility.
249: What feature is associated with a temperature inversion?
A stable layer of air. An unstable layer of air. Chinook winds on mountain slopes. Air mass thunderstorms. 250: Which weather conditions would be expected beneath a low-level
temperature inversion layer when the relative humidity is high?
Smooth air and poor visibility due to fog, haze, or low clouds. Light wind shear and poor visibility due to haze and light rain. Turbulent air and poor visibility due to fog, low stratus type clouds, and showery precipitation.
Updrafts and turbulence due to surface heating, fair visibility, and cumulus clouds developing at the top of the inversion.

Wind shear. Rubbing two objects together creates friction. If the
objects are fluid or air currents, friction creates eddies along a common
shallow mixing zone, and a mass transfer takes place in the shallow mixing
layer. A relatively steep gradient in wind velocity along a given line or
direction (Either vertical or horizontal) produces churning motions
(eddies) which result in turbulence. The greater the change of wind speed
and/or direction in the given direction, the more severe the turbulence.
This zone of induced eddies and mixing is a shear zone. Wind shear is best
described as a change in wind direction and/or speed within a very short
distance in the atmosphere.
Wind shear may be associated with either a wind shift or a wind speed
gradient at any level in the atmosphere. Wind shear associated with a
low-level temperature inversion occurs when wind just above the inversion
is relatively strong; a wind shear zone develops between the calm and the
stronger winds above. Eddies in the shear zone cause airspeed fluctuations
as an aircraft climbs or decends through the inversion. When airspeed is
at a low margin above stall speed, such as during approach or departure,
the fluctuations in airspace can induce a stall precariously close to the
ground.
251: Where does wind shear occur?
At any level, and it can exist in both a horizontal and vertical direction.
Only at higher altitudes, usually in the vicinity of jet streams. Primarily at lower altitudes in the vicinity of mountain waves. Only in the vicinity of thunderstorms.

When taking off or landing in calm wind under clear skies within a few
hours before or after sunrise, be prepared for a temperature inversion near
the ground. You can be relatively certain of a shear zone in the inversion
if you know the wind at 2000 to 4000 feet is 25 knots or more. Allow a
margin of airspeed above normal climb or approach speed to alleviate danger
of stall in event of turbulence or sudden change in wind velocity.
252: A pilot can expect a wind shear zone in a temperature inversion,
whenever the wind speed at 2000 to 4000 feet above the surface is at least:
25 kts 5 kts 10 kts 15 kts 253: If there is thunderstorm activity in the vicinity of an airport at which
you plan to land, which hazardous and invisible atmospheric phenomenon might
be expected to on the landing approach.
Wind shear turbulence St. Elmo's fire Tornadoes Virga 254: Hazardous wind shear is commonly encountered near the ground:
During periods of strong temperature inversion and near thunderstorms. Near thunderstorms and during periods when the wind velocity is stronger than 35 kts.
During periods when the wind velocity is stronger than 35kts and near mountain valleys.
Near mountain valleys and on the windward side of a hill or mountain.

Wave action also may occur when the air is too dry to form clouds.
Thus, the pilot should analyze weather reports, forecasts, and obtain reports
to help avoid a wave since clouds may not be present to indicate the location
of wave activity.
255: An almond or lens-shapped cloud which appears stationary, but which may
contain winds of 50kts or more, is referred to as:
A lenticular cloud. An inactive frontal cloud. A funnel cloud. A stratus cloud. 256: Crests of standing mountain waves may be marked by stationary, lens-shaped
clouds known as:
Standing lenticular clouds. Cumulonimbus mamma clouds. Roll clouds. Rotor clouds. 257: The conditions necessary for the formation of cumulonimbus clouds are
lifting action and:
Unstable, moist air. Unstable air containing an excess of condensation nuclei. Either stable or unstable air. Stable, moist air. 258: What conditions are necessary for the formation of thunderstorms?
Lifting force, high humidity, and unstable conditions. High humidity, high temperature, and cumulus clouds. Low pressure, high humidity, and cumulus clouds. Lifting force, high temperature, and unstable conditions. 259: What feature is normally associated with the cumulus stage of a
thunderstorm?
Continuous updraft. Roll cloud. Frequent lightning. Beginning of rain at the surface. 260: Which weather phenomenon signals the beginning of the mature stage of a
thunderstorm?
The start of rain at the surface. The appearnce of an anvil top. Growth rate of cloud is maximum. Strong turbulence in the cloud. 261: Thunderstorms reach their greatest intensity during the
Mature stage. Updraft stage. Downdraft stage. Cumulus stage. 262: During the life cycle of a thunderstorm, which stage is characterized
predominately by downdrafts?
Dissipating Cumulus Mature Anvil 263: A nonfrontal, narrow band of active thunderstorms that often develop
ahead of a cold front is known as:
A squall line. An occlusion. A prefrontal system. A shear line. 264: Thunderstorms which generally produce the most intense hazard to
aircraft are:
Squall line thunderstorms. Air mass thunderstorms. Steady thunderstorms. Warm front thunderstorms. 265: Which weather phenomenon is always associated with a thunderstorm?
Lightning. Heavy rain showers. Supercooled raindrops. Hail.

Aircraft icing is one of the major weather hazards to aviation. Icing is a
cumulative hazard. It reduces aircraft efficiency by increasing weither,
reducing lift, decreasing thrust, and increasing drag. Icing also
seriously impairs aircraft engine performance.
Two conditions are necessary for structural icing in flight; (1) the
aircraft must be flying through visible liquid moisture such as rain or
cloud droplets, and (2) the temperature at the point where the moisture
strikes the aircraft must be 0"C or cooler.
266: An inflight condition necessary for structural icing to form is:
Visibile moisture Cumuliform clouds Cirrostratus clouds Stratiform clouds

Freezing rain. Freezing rain or drizzle may be encountered in clear air
below a cloud deck. When warm, moist air is forced to rise over a colder
air mass, a frontal inversion will exist. Below this inversion, icing
dangers are frequently encountered. In beforeced aloft, the air may be
sufficiently cooled to produce saturation and precipitation. The rain or
drizzle falling into the colder air should be expected to freeze upon
contact with the aircraft if the temperature is 2"C or below. Freezing
rain is the most dangerous icing condition; it can build to enormous
proportions in a few minutes and is extremely difficult to break loose.
267: In which environment is aircraft structural ice most likely to have
the highest accumulation rate?
Freezing rain. Cumulus clouds. Cirrus clouds. Stratus clouds.

When raindrops fall through the air that is below freezing, they may freeze
to form ice pellets. This form of ice is a cold weather phenomenon. It
does not bring about the formation of structural ice, except when it is
mixed with supercooled water. However, the presense of ice pellets at a
given level in the atmosphere is a good indicator of freezing rain at some
higher altitude.
268: The presense of ice pellets at the surface is evidence that:
There is freezing rain at higher altitudes There are thunderstorms in the area A cold front has passed The pilot can climb to a higher altitude without encountering more than light icing.

Frost forms in much the same way as dew. The only difference is that the
dewpoint of the surrounding air must be colder than freezing. Water vapor
then sublimates directly as ice crystals or frost rather than condensing as
dew.
269: Which conditions result in the formation of frost?
The temperature of the collecting surface is at or below the dew point of the adjacent air and the dew point is below freezing.
The freezing of dew. The collecting surface's temperature is at or below freezing and small droplets of moisture fall on the collected surface.
Small drops of moisture falling on the collecting surface when the surrounding air temperature is at or below freezing.

Frost does not change the basic aerodynamic shape of the wing, but the
roughness of its surface spoils the smooth flow of air, thus causing a
slowing of the airflow. This slowing of the airflow causes early airflow
separation over the affected airfoil resulting in a loss of lift. Even a
small amount of frost on airfoils may prevent an aircraft from becoming
airborne at normal takeoff speed. Also possible is that, once airborne, an
aircraft could have insufficient margin of airspeed above stall so that
moderage gusts or turning flight could produce incipient or complete
stalling.
270: Why is frost considered hazardous to flight operation?
Frost causes early airflow separation resulting in a loss of lift. The increased weight requires a greater takeoff distance. Frost changes the basic aerodynamic shape of the airfoil. Frost decreases control effectiveness. 271: Frost which has not been removed from the lifting surfaces of an
airplane before flight:
May prevent the airplane from becoming airborne. Will change the camber (Curvature of the wing) thereby increasing lift during the takeoff.
May cause the airplane to become airborne with a lower angle of attack and at a lower indicated airspeed.
Would present no problems since frost will blow off when the airplane starts moving during takeoff.
272: Frost which has not been removed from the wings of an airplane before
flight:
May make it difficult or impossible to become airborne. May cause the airplane to become airborne with a lower angle of attack and at a lower indicated airspeed.
Would present no problems since frost will blow off when the airplane starts moving during takeoff.
Will change the camber (Curvature of the wing) thereby increasing lift during takeoff.

Large high pressure systems frequently stagnate over large areas of land or
water with relatively uniform surface conditions. They take on
characteristics of these "source regions", the coldness of polar regions,
the heat of the tropics, the moisture of oceans, or the dryness of
continents.
As they move away from their source regions and pass over land or sea, the
air masses are constantly being modified through ehating or cooling from
below, lifting or subsiding, absorbing or losing moisture. Actual
temperature of the air mass is less important than its temperature in
relation to the land or water surface over which it is passing. For
example, an air mass moving from polar regions usually is colder than the
land and sea surfaces over which it passes. On the other hand, an air mass
moving from the gulf of Mexico in winter usually is warmer than the
territory over which it passes.
If the air is colder than the surface, it will be warmed from below and
convection currents will be set up, causing turbulence. Dust, smoke, and
atmospheric pollution near the ground will be carried upward by these
currents and dissipated at higher levels, improving surface visibility.
Such air is called "unstable".
Conversely, if the air is warmer than the surface, there is no tendency for
convection currents to form, and the air is smooth. Smoke, dust, etc. are
concentrated in lower levels with resulting poor visibility. Such air is
called "stable".
273: Which of the following would decrease the stability of an airmass?
Warming from below. Cooling from below. Decrease in water vapor. Sinking of the airmass.

Thus broad and generalized conclusions can be drawn in regard to the
stability of the atmosphere. However, precise atmospheric temperature
moisture soundings must be considered to determine whether the air is
actually stable or unstable. The density differences that cause a parcel
of air to be eitgher stable or unstable are the result of the differences
between the environmental lapse rate (or the vertical temperature profile
of the atmosphere) and the adiabatic lapse rate. The adiabatic lapse rate
is the temperature change within a parcel of air that is accelerated
vertically.
274: Which of the following measurements can be used to determine the stability
of the atmosphere?
Actual lapse rate Atmospheric pressure Surface temperature Wind velocity 275: An unstable air mass is most likely to have which of the following
characteristics?
Turbulent air. Stratiform clouds and fog. Continuous precipitation. Fair to poor visibility in haze and smoke. 276: What are the characteristics of unstable air?
Turbulence and good surface visibility. Turbulence and poor surface visibility. Nimbostratus clouds and good surface visibility. Nimbostratus clouds and poor surface visibility. 277: A moist, unstable air mass is characterized by:
Cumuliform clouds and showery precipitation. Poor visibility and smooth air. Stratiform clouds and continuous precipitation. Fog and drizzle. 278: A stable air mass is most likely to have which of the following
characteristics?
Smooth air. Showery precipitation. Turbulent air. Cumuliform clouds. 279: What is a characteristic of stable air?
Stratiform clouds. Unlimited visibilty. Fair weather cumulus clouds. Temperature decreases rapidly with altitude. 280: Steady precipitation, in contrast to showery, preceding a front is an
indication of:
Stratiform clouds with little or no turbulence. Cumuliform clouds with moderate turbulence. Stratiform clouds with moderate turbulence. Cumuliform clouds with little or no turbulence. 281: The boundary between two different air masses is referred to as a:
Front Foehn gap. Frontolysis Frontogenesis 282: One weather phenemenon which will always occur when flying across a
front is:
A change in the wind. A large precipitation area, if the frontal surface is steep. A large temperature change, especially at high altitudes. The presense of clouds, either ahead of or behind the front. 288: Radar weather reports are of special interest to pilots because they
report:
Location of precipitation along with type, intensity, and trend. Large areas of low ceilings and fog. Location of broken to overcast clouds. Icing conditions. 289: What information is provided by the radar summary chart that is not
shown on other weather charts?
Lines and cells of hazardous thunderstorms. Ceilings and precipitation between reporting stations. Types of precipitation between reporting stations. Areas of cloud cover and icing levels within the clouds. 290: For what phase of flight planning are the significant weather progs
designed?
Information to avoid areas and/or altitudes of most significant icing and turbulence.
A complete set of weather forecasts for overall planning. An analysis of frontal activity, cloud coverage, and areas of precipitation.
Overall depicition of current ceilings and visibilities with fronts and icing levels.

"Ceiling" is defined as:
(1) Height of the lowest layer of clouds or obscuring phenomena aloft that
is reported as broken or overcast and not classified as thin; or
(2) Vertical visibility into a surface based obscuring phenomena that hides
all the sky.
294: Ceiling, as used in weather reports, is defined as the height above
the Earth's surface of the:
Lowest layer of clouds reported as broken or overcast and not classified as thin.
Lowest reported obscuration and the highest layer of clouds reported as overcast.
Lowest layer of clouds reported as scattered, broken, or thin. Highest layer of clouds reported as broken or thin.

Sky conditions and celing: The sky condition is reported in SA's using the
designators shown in the table:
Code Meaning Spoken
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
CLR Clear (less than 0.1 sky cover) Clear
SCT Scattered layer aloft (0.1 to 0.5) Scattered
BKN Broken layer aloft (0.6 to 0.9), considered ceiling Broken
OVC Overcase layer (0.9 to 1.0), considered ceiling Overcase
X Sky obscured by some surface phenomena Sky obscured
-X Sky partially obscured by some surface phenomena Sky partially
obscured
Weather and obstructions:
Code Spoken
---------------------------
Tornado Tornado
Funnel cloud Funnel cloud
Waterspout Waterspout
T Thunderstorm
R Rain
Z Freezing
W Showers
I Ice
C Crystals
P Pellets
S Snow
A Hail
L Drizzle
B Blowing
D Dust
N Sand
Y Spray
F Fog
GF Ground fog
H Haze
K Smoke
+ Heavy
- Light (partial)

INK SA 1854 CLR 15 106/77/63/1112G18/000
BOI SA 1854 150 SCT 30 181/62/42/1304/015
LAX SA 1852 7 SCT 250 SCT 6HK 129/60/59/2504/991
MDW RS 1856 -X M7 OVC 11/2R+F 990/63/61/3205/980/RF2 RB12
JFK RS 1853 W5 X 1/2F 180/68/64/1804/006/RO4RVR22V30 TWR VSBY 1/4
295: What is the sky condition depicted for Chicago Midway Airport (MDW)?
Sky partially obscured, measured ceiling 700 overcast, visibility 1-1/2, heavy rain, fog.
Thin overcast, cealing 700 feet, overcast 1100 feet, visibility 2 miles in rain plus fog.
Thin overcast measured 700 feet overcast, visibility 1-1/2, heavy rain, fog.
Sky partially obscured, measured ceiling 700 overcast, visibility 11, occasionally 2, with rain and heavy fog.
296: Which of the reporting stations in figure 9 have VFR weather?
All except JFK and MDW All All except JFK INK only 297: The wind direction and velocity at JFK is from:
180" at 4kts 180" at 40kts 040" at 22 to 30 kts 018" at 4 to 6kts 298: What are the wind conditions at Wink, Texas (INK)?
110", 12kts, gusting to 18kts Calm 111" gusting to 18kts 011", 12kts gusting to 18kts 299: From which primary source should information be obtained regarding
expected weather at your destination and estimated time of arrival?
Terminal forecast Low level prog. chart. Weather depiction chart. Radar Summary and Weather Depiction Chart

OK FT 011447
GAG FT 011515 100 SCT 250 SCT 2610. 16Z 60 SCT C100 BKN 3315G22 CHC C50 BKN
5TRW. 01Z 250 SCT 3515G25. 09Z VFR WIND..
HBR FT 011515 C120 BKN 250 BKN 3010. 17Z 100 SCT C250 BKN 3215G25 CHC
C30 BKN 3TRW. 00Z 250 SCT 3515G25. 092 VFR WIND..
MLC FT 011515 C20 BKN 1815 BKN OCNL SCT. 20Z C30 BKN 1815G22 CHC C20 BKN
1TRW. 03Z C30 BKN 2015 CHC C7 X 1/2TRW+G40. 09Z MFVR CIG TRW..
OKC FT 011515 C12 BKN 140 BKN 1815G28 LWR BKN V SCT. 18Z C30 BKN 250 BKN
2315G25 LWR BKN OCNL SCT CHC C7 X 1/2TRW+G40. 21Z CFP 100 SCT C250
BKN 3315G25 CHC C30 BKN 5TRW-. 02Z 100 SCT 250 SCT 3515G25. 09Z VFR WIND..
PNC FT 011515 C100 BKN 250 BKN 1810. 16Z CFP 20 SCT C100 BKN 3115 SCT V
BKN. 00Z 250 SCT 3515G25. 09Z VFR WIND..
TUL FT 011515 C20 BKN 1915G22. 19Z C30 BKN 1815G25 CHC 3TRW. 23Z CFP C100
BKN 250 BKN 3215G25 CHC C30 BKN 5TRW. 09Z VFR WIND..
300: What ceiling is forecast for Gage (GAG) between 16Z and midnight Z?
10000 broken. 100 scattered. 6000 scattered. 1000 broken. 301: What wind conditions are expected at Hobart (HBR) at 16Z?
300" at 10kts Calm 115" at 15kts 320" at 15kts gusting to 25kts 302: According to the terminal forecast for OKC, the cold front should pass
through:
between 21Z and 02Z the next day. Between 18Z and 21Z with heavy thunderstorms. Between 1515Z and 18Z. After 09Z the next day. 303: When is the wind forecast to shift at Tulsa (TUL)?
Between 19Z and 23Z. 15Z. Between 23Z and 09Z the next day. After 09Z the next day. 304: What is the outlook for weather conditions at McAlester (MLC)?
Marginal VFR due to low ceilings and thundershowers Ceilings 2000-3000 feet with southerly winds. Ceilings 700 feet, sky obscured, visibility 1/2 mile in thundershowers VFR except in the thundershowers, peak wind gusts 40 kts 305: The wind condition in the terminal forecast outlook for Ponca City (PNC)?
For velocities of 25kts or stronger. Missing. For a wind shift from south to northwest. For the wind to change from a gusty condition to calm. 306: To best determine forecast weather conditions between weather reporting
stations, the pilot should refer to:
Area forecasts. Pilot reports. Prognostic charts. Weather maps. 307: To determine the freezing level and areas of probable icing aloft, the
pilot should refer to the:
Area forecast. Radar summary chart. Weather depiction chart. Surface analysis. 312: What information is contained in a convective SIGMET in the
conterminous United States?
Tornadoes, embedded thunderstorms, and hail 3/4 in or greater in diameter. Severe icing, severe turbulence, or widespread dust storms lowering visibility to less than three miles.
Weather less than basic VFR and sustained winds of 30 kts or greater at the surface.
Ceilings less than 500 feet and visibility less than 1 mile. 313: SIGMET's are issued as a warning of weather conditions hazardous
To all aircraft Particularly to light aircraft Only to light aircraft operations Particularly to heavy aircraft 314: Which inflight advisory would contain information on severe icing?
SIGMET Convective SIGMET AIRMET PIREP 315: Which in-flight advisory would contain information on severe icing?
Thunderstorms are obscured by massive cloud layers and cannot be seen. Thunderstorms have been visually sighted. Severe thunderstorms are embedded within a squall line Thunderstorms are dissipating and present no serious problem to IFR flight.
316: AIRMET's are issued as a warning of weather conditions hazardous
Particularly to light airplanes. To all airplanes to VFR operations only Particularly to heavy airplanes

Winds and temperatures aloft forecast:
The winds and temperatures aloft are forecast for specific locations in the
contiguous United States. These forecasts are also prepared for a network
of locations in Alaska. Forecasts are made twice daily based on 0000Z and
1200Z data for use during specific time intervals.
Figure 12 is an example of a winds and temperatures aloft forecast (FD)
containing the heading and six FD locations. The heading always includes
the time during which the FD may be used (1900-0300Z) in the example) and a
notation "TEMPS NEG ABV 24000". Since temperatures above 24000 feet are
always negative, the minus sign is omitted.
Forecast levels. The line labelled "FT" shows 9 of 11 standard FD levels.
The 45000 and 53000 foot levels are not transmitted on teletypewriter
circuits but are available in the communications system. The pilot may
request these levels from the FSS briefer or NWS meteorologist. Through
12000 feet the levels are true altitude, 18000 feet and above are pressue
altitude. The station identifiers denoting the location for which the
forecast applies are arranged in alphabetical order in a column along the
left side of the data sheet. The coded wind and temperature information in
digits is found in columns under each level and in the line to the right of
the station identifier.
Note that some lower level groups are omitted. No winds are forecast
within 1500 feet of station elevation. No temperatures are forecast for
the 3000 feet level or for a level within 2500 feet of the station
elevation.
Decoding: Note that wind direction and speed are jammed together with
the direction in tens of degrees. 0507 means wind at 50" from true north,
7 knots.

FD WBC 151745
BASED ON 151200Z DATA
VALID 1600Z FOR USE 1800-0300Z. TEMPS NEG ABV 24000
FT 3000 6000 9000 12000 18000 24000 30000 34000 39000
ALA 2420 2635-08 2535-18 2444-30 245945 246755 246862
AMA 2714 2725+00 2625-04 2531-15 2542-27 265842 256352 256762
DEN 2321-04 2532-08 2434-19 2441-31 235347 236056 236262
HLC 1701-01 2113-03 2219-07 2330-17 2435-30 244145 244854 245561
MKC 0507 2006+03 2215-01 2322-06 2338-17 2348-29 236143 237252 238160
STL 2113 2325+07 2332+02 2339-04 2356-16 2373-27 239440 730649 731960
317: What values are used for Winds Aloft forecasts?
True direction and knots Magnetic direction and knots Magnetic direction and miles per hour True direction and miles per hour 318: Interpret the wind and temperature aloft forecast for 3000 feet at
Kansas City (MKC).
050" at 7Kts, temperature missing. 360" at 5kts, temperature -7"C. 360" at 50kts, temperature at +7"C. 005" at 7kts, temperature missing.

A 6-digit group includes the forecast temperature aloft. Refer to the Kansas
(KMC) forecast for the 6000 foot level. The group "2006+03" means the wind
is forecast from 200" at 6kts with a temperature of +3"C.
319: What wind is forecast for St. Louis (STL) at 18000 feet?
230" at 56kts 030" at 56kts 235" at 06kts 235" at 6 gusting to 16kts 320: Determine the wind and temperature aloft forecast for Denver (DEN) at
30000 feet.
230" at 53kts, -47"C. 23" at 53kts, 45"C. 235" at 34kts, -7"C. 235" at 34kts, -47"C. 321: Individual forecasts for specific routes of flight can be obtained
from which weather service?
Transcribed weather broadcasts. Terminal forecasts. Area forecasts. In flight advisories. 322: To obtain a continuous transcribed weather briefing including winds aloft
and route forecasts for a cross country flight, a pilot should monitor:
A TWEB on a low-frequency radio receiver A VHF radio receiver tuend to an ATIS frequency The regularly scheduled weather broadcast on a VOR frequency A high-frequency radio receiver tuned to the en-route Flight Advisory Service (Flight watch) station.
323: TWEB's (Transcribed Weather Broadcasts) may be monitored by tuning the
appropriate radio receiver to certain
VOR and NDB frequencies FSS communications frequencies Airport advisory frequencies NDB frequencies only

To contact a flight watch facility on 122.0MHz use the name of the VOR
nearest to your location, followed by the words "FLIGHT WATCH". The flight
watch facility that provides service that area will respond to your call by
the facility name. If you know in whose flight watch area you are flying,
you can use the name of the facility, followed by an abbreviated position
report to assist the operator in selecting an appropriate communications
outlet.
331: Below FL180, enroute weather advisories should be obtained from an FSS
on:
122.0 MHz 122.1 MHZ 123.6 MHz 122.4 MHz 332: On what frequency can a pilot receive enroute flight advisory service
in the area depicted by figure A-4?
122.0 MHz 117.1 MHz 118.0 MHz 122.96 MHz 334: What service should a pilot normally expect from an En Route Flight
Advisory Service station?
Actual weather information and thunderstorm activity along the route. Preferential routing and provide radar vectoring to circumnavigate severe weather.
Severe weather information, changes to flight plans, and receive routine position reports.
Radar vectors for traffic avoidance, routine weather advisories and altimeter settings.
335: How should you establish contact with an EFAS (En Route Flight Advisory
Service) station, and what service would you normally expect?
Call flight watch on 122.0 for information regarding actual weather and thunderstorm activity along your route.
Call EFAS on 122.2 for routine weather, current reports on hazardous weather, and altimeter settings.
Call flight assistance on 122.5 for advisory service pertaining to severe weather.
Call ARTCC on asigned frequency and ask for Flight Watch services.

Telephone Weather Briefing. When telephoning for a briefing, use the
following procedure:
(1) Identify yourself as a pilot.
(2) State your intended route, destination, proposed departure time,
estimated time enroute and type of aircraft.
(3) Advise if you intend to fly IFR or VFR.
(4) When talking to an FSS, you will be asked your aircraft identification
for record purposes. With this background, the briefer can proceed
directly with the briefing and concentrate on weather relevant to your
flight. The weather briefing you receive should include:
Hazardous weather if any (you may elect to cancel at this point)
Weather synopsis (position of lows, fronts, etc.).
Forecast (enroute and destination).
Alternate routes (if any).
Forecast winds aloft.
336: When telephoning a weather briefing facility for preflight weather
information, pilots should
Identify themselves as pilots Tell the number of hours they have flowin within the preceding 90 days. State the number of occupants on board and the color of the aircraft. State that they possess a current medical certificate. 337: When telephoning a weather briefing facility for preflight weather
information, pilots should state:
Your intended route, destination and type of aircraft. That you possess a current medical certificate. The color of the aircraft and number of occupants on board. Your total flight time. 338: When telephoning a weather briefing facility for preflight weather
information, pilots should state
Whether you intend to fly VFR only. The number of hours you have flowin within the preceding 90 days. That you posses a current medical certificate. The color of the aircraft and the number of occupants on board.

The FSSs and WSOs are to serve you. You should not hesitate to discuss
factors that need elaboration or to ask questions. You have a complete
briefing only when you have a clear picture of the weather to expect. It
is to your advantage to make a final weather check immediately before
departure if at all possible. A sample weather briefing is provided you in
Figure 14.

This is a pilot weather briefing from the FSS at Boston Massachusetts, for
a flight to Buffalo, New York. The pilot advises that the departing time
will be 18Z and the ETA is approximately 3 hours.
'Conditions are for marginal VFR and occasionally IFR conditions for the
entire flight. Delta Two calls for occasional moderate icing in the clouds
and precipitation from Buffalo to central Massachusetts from 4 thousand to
6 thousand. SIGMET NINE BRAVO warns of scattered embedded thundershowers
along coastal Massachusetts continuing beyond 18Z.'
'At 12Z a warm front extended from a low centered near Toledo, Ohio,
eastward accross central Pennsylvania and into the Atlantic just south of
Long Island.'
'Current weather here at Boston is 3 thousand scattered, ceiling 4 thousand
overcast, 6 miles in light rain and fog, temperature 43, dew point 39 wind
110 at 15, altimeter 29 point 90. Here at Boston at departure time, we
should have 2 thousand scattered 3 thousand 5 hundred overcast, 5 miles in
light rain and fog.'
'You should experience VFR conditions through eastern and central
Massachusetts with marginal and occasionally IFR conditions from western
Massachussetts through central New York and VFR the remainder of the route.
Currently, Buffalo is reporting 15 hundred overcast, 5 miles visibility in
light rain and fog, wind 090 at 10 gusting to 20.'
'En route, clouds are layered from 1 thousand to above 25 thousand.
Visibilities will be 5 miles, occasionally 3 miles in precipitation and
fog. AIRMET DELTA TWO warns of occasionally moderate ixed icing in the
clouds and precipitation from 4 to 6 thousand from New York to central
Massachusetts. Convective SIGMET NINE BRAVO forecasts a freezing layer, 4
to 6 thousand New York to central Massachusetts, otherwise the freezing
level is 12thousand at Buffalo sloping to 14 thousand at Boston. At 1435Z
the pilot of a B747 reported mixed icing during descent from 6 thousand to
4 thousand.'
'At 21Z Buffalo should have 12 hundred overcast, 5 miles visibility in
light rain and fog.'
'Winds aloft forecast; From Boston to Albany, 3 thousand 150 at 18, 6
thousand 160 at 35, 9 thousand 270 at 35, 12 thousand and above 270 at 45.
From Albany to Buffalo, 3 thousand 100 at 18, 6 thousand 120 at 30, 9
thousand and above 240 at 35 to 45.'
339: What is the widespread system affecting the enroute weather described in
the weather briefing in figure 14?
Warm front. Temperature inversion. Low pressure trough. Occluded front. 340: Which portion of the route indicates questionable VFR conditions?
Western Massachusetts through central New York. Eastern Massachusetts to the New York border. Central New York through to Buffalo. In the Buffalo area. 341: What change in weather conditions is indicated at Boston from the time
of the briefing until departure time?
Second layer of clouds and visibility lowering. IFR to VFR conditions Rain decreasing with pressure and temperature remaining stable. Lowering of ceiling and visibility to 1000 feet, and 3 miles in thundershowers.
342: Where is there a possibility of turbulence on the proposed route?
In the Boston area. Below the freezing level along the entire route. Between 12000 and 14000 feet. Albany to New York. 343: Why are there two separate levels of freezing temperatures reported?
Freezing temperatures at one level on each side of the temperature inversion.
Freezing temperatures from 4000 feet up reported at the cloud layers only. Only the levels where airframe icing has been forecast or reported by pilots.
One layer contains conditions for airframe icing and the other contains conditions for carburetor icing.
